name: Fox Mine & Other Mines
short_description: BLM public land near Elko offering recreational gold and quartz collecting. Flat terrain at nearly 6000 feet elevation makes this an accessible spot for rockhounders seeking Nevada's historic minin...
description:
Fox Mine and the surrounding mines in Elko County represent some of Nevada's most accessible public rockhounding opportunities. Located on BLM land at 5917 feet elevation, this site offers relatively flat terrain that makes it suitable for collectors of varying experience levels. The area has a rich mining history tied to Nevada's gold and silver booms, with numerous historical workings scattered throughout the region.

The geology of this area reflects the Basin and Range province, where Tertiary-aged quartz veins and associated mineralization were formed through hydrothermal processes during the Cenozoic Era. These quartz-dominated systems often contain fine gold specimens, chalcedony, and other silica-rich minerals typical of the Great Basin's productive mining districts. The flat topography combined with exposed outcrops makes it relatively easy to identify promising collecting areas.

Collectors here typically find small gold flakes and flour gold in quartz matrix, translucent to milky quartz specimens, and occasional chalcedony formations. The site is best suited for those interested in specimens over large quantities, though the 25-pound daily limit provides reasonable flexibility for the casual rockhound.

As BLM land, this location requires no permit and allows year-round access for personal use collecting. The relatively flat terrain and reasonable elevation make it accessible during the cooler months when Nevada's high desert conditions are most comfortable for extended fieldwork.
history: The Fox Mine area sits within the larger Elko County mining district, where Tertiary-aged hydrothermal systems created quartz veins and disseminated gold mineralization during the Cenozoic Era. Historical mining activity in this region dates to Nevada's gold and silver booms, with numerous small mines and prospect holes reflecting over a century of exploration by miners seeking the productive ore bodies that characterize the Great Basin's fault-block geology.
tips: Search along exposed quartz outcrops and in small washes where weathering has released specimens from the surrounding rock. Bring a 10x loupe to examine quartz for gold flakes and to identify chalcedony color variations. Focus on areas with visible iron staining or oxidized zones, which often indicate more productive mineralized ground. The flat terrain allows you to cover ground efficiently, but scan the surface carefully before digging, as many good specimens are found loose on the surface.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently sloping terrain at 5917 feet elevation. High desert basin topography with sparse vegetation typical of the Great Basin. Exposed rock outcrops and small wash systems provide good access for surface collecting. Walking distance from parking is generally short with good footing on compacted dirt and small rock fragments. Terrain is easily walkable but brings sturdy hiking boots to protect feet from sharp quartz fragments.
best_season: October - April. Nevada's high desert elevations (nearly 6000 feet here) are most comfortable during cooler months. Summer temperatures in this region exceed 85 degrees Fahrenheit regularly, making spring and fall ideal. Winter is possible but occasional snow at this elevation may limit access.
written_directions: From Elko, take US 93 south approximately 25 miles toward the Nevada-Utah border. Watch for BLM public land markers and dirt turnoffs in the Elko County mining area. The Fox Mine is accessible via rough dirt roads branching east from the highway near mile marker signs typical of the region. Park at a suitable pullout or flat area near visible outcrops. Local BLM office in Elko can provide more detailed directions and current road conditions.
road_conditions: Rough dirt roads with possible washboard surface. High-clearance vehicle recommended but passenger cars may access in dry conditions. Check with local BLM office for current road conditions.
parking_notes: Pullouts and flat areas along dirt roads near mining sites. No developed parking lot. Park away from active claim areas and respect any posted mining claims.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Respect all posted mining claims - do not trespass on private claims. Collect loose specimens only unless specifically on your own claim. Pack out all trash and leave no trace.
rules:
Personal use collecting only
Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection permitted
No motorized excavation allowed
Respect posted mining claims
Collect loose specimens only
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Elko, Nevada, approximately 25 miles north. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water available in town.
accessibility_notes: Flat terrain at high elevation makes this accessible to most hikers and collectors. Rough dirt roads to parking areas require high-clearance vehicles. No wheelchair accessibility to collecting areas. Sturdy footwear essential due to sharp quartz fragments scattered throughout the site. Distance from parking to productive collecting areas is generally short, making this suitable for families and those with limited mobility who can walk short distances on uneven ground.
alternative_names: Basin and Range Quartz Mining Area

faq.question: What can I find rockhounding at Fox Mine near Elko, Nevada?
faq.answer: Fox Mine and surrounding claims yield fine gold flakes, flour gold in quartz matrix, translucent to milky quartz specimens, and chalcedony formations. Most specimens are small to medium-sized pieces suitable for display or jewelry work. Surface collecting often rewards careful observation with loose specimens that have weathered from quartz veins.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Fox Mine on BLM land in Nevada?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use collecting on this BLM land near Elko. You may collect year-round as long as you follow the 25-pound daily limit per FLPMA rules and respect all posted mining claims. Always check with the local BLM office before visiting to confirm access and current conditions.

faq.question: What is the best season for rockhounding at Fox Mine, Nevada?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. The high desert elevation of nearly 6000 feet makes summer temperatures uncomfortable for extended fieldwork. Spring and fall offer perfect conditions for several hours of surface collecting. Winter is possible but occasional snow may limit road access.

faq.question: Is Fox Mine rockhounding site beginner friendly for families with kids?
faq.answer: Yes. The flat terrain and short walking distances make this accessible for children ages 6 and up. The site requires no technical skills - surface collecting of loose specimens is the primary activity. Bring plenty of water, sun protection, and teach kids to watch for sharp quartz fragments. The high desert environment is educational and relatively safe for supervised family collecting trips.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Fox Mine, Nevada?
faq.answer: Bring a 10x loupe to examine quartz for gold and identify chalcedony color variations. A small hammer, chisel, and sturdy backpack are useful for collecting. Wear hiking boots to protect from sharp quartz fragments. Pack plenty of water (at least 2 gallons), sunscreen, a hat, and a head lamp. Since cell service is spotty, bring a map or GPS unit.

faq.question: How far is Fox Mine rockhounding site from Elko, Nevada and what are road conditions?
faq.answer: Fox Mine is approximately 25 miles south of Elko via US 93. Access roads are rough dirt tracks requiring high-clearance vehicles. Passenger cars may reach some areas in dry conditions. Road conditions vary seasonally, so check with the Elko BLM office before your trip. The site is relatively remote but accessible for serious rockhounders with appropriate vehicles.

meta_title: Fox Mine Rockhounding - Nevada Gold & Quartz
meta_description: Find gold, quartz, and chalcedony at Fox Mine near Elko, Nevada. Free BLM public land collecting with no permit required. October-April.
